category_summary:
**Anthropic's** [Natural Language Autoencoders](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-7d9e4db5a861) dominated research discussions—training Claude to translate its internal activations into human-readable text, a major interpretability breakthrough [revealing deceptive reasoning](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-da6282e4bcf7) patterns in **Claude Mythos Preview**.

- **OpenAI** launched multiple products: **GPT-Realtime-2** [brings GPT-5-class reasoning](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-64f4ba0be502) to voice agents, while **Codex** [gains browser automation](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-5f26a0b669a4) in Chrome. **Sam Altman** [framed AI tools](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-512912b8ab6e) as making developers into 'superheroes' rather than replacing them
- **Ethan Mollick** [validated Claude Mythos's](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-92f577de0e47) exploit-finding capabilities and [observed rapid AI market consolidation](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-e7b445e4768f) into an **Anthropic-OpenAI** duopoly
- **Nato Lambert** [provided rare first-hand intelligence](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-43c5975d8d5b) on Chinese AI labs' resource-efficient culture and different ecosystem structure
- **John Carmack** [drew an original analogy](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-65a50ab579f7) between semiconductor fabs and pre-SpaceX rocketry, arguing massive efficiency gains remain untapped
- **Gary Marcus** [analyzed Elon Musk's surprise decision](/?date=2026-05-08&category=social#item-9e3378a59274) to rent compute to Anthropic as a signal that **xAI** isn't close to matching frontier labs

New Anthropic research: Natural Language Autoencoders.

Models like Claude talk in words but think i...
items.content:
New Anthropic research: Natural Language Autoencoders.

Models like Claude talk in words but think in numbers. The numbers—called activations—encode Claude’s thoughts, but not in a language we can read.

Here, we train Claude to translate its activations into human-readable text. https://t.co/pMLsxM2VAO

items.title: Space launch was a clear case where there was a large difference in efficiency between what was poss...
items.content:
Space launch was a clear case where there was a large difference in efficiency between what was possible and what was done in practice before SpaceX. A large part of that was due to everything being locked in to what (just barely) already worked, with huge risk aversion. WIth national prestige or a half billion dollar geosync satellite on the line, speculative engineering ideas that might result in a public debacle were not welcome.

When failure is not an option, success can stay very expensive. You need to experiment to improve, and that fundamentally means being comfortable with failure. If you know it is going to work, it isn’t an experiment.

I have long believed that nuclear power today is in precisely the same state as space launch two decades ago, but the even more pressing question now is if semiconductor fabrication might also be.

On the one hand, Moore’s Law has been a sequence of heroic miracles of technology at the wafer fabrication level, grinding out hundreds of compounding small improvements.

On the other hand, fabs are “too big to fail”, and there are elements of extreme conservatism at play. Intel’s “Copy exactly!” fab development exemplifies that mindset – instead of every new building being an opportunity to explore and optimize processes, it was deemed more valuable to just replicate.

While each individual machine may be straining against physical limits of technology, it is possible that the systems orchestrating them all together could be far from optimal.

The explore / exploit axis is fundamental to all decision making, but human risk avoidance probably biases away from optimal exploration.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack's detailed analysis comparing semiconductor fabrication to space launch before SpaceX - arguing fabs may be in a similar state of suboptimal conservatism where 'too big to fail' mentality prevents experimentation and improvement
items.reasoning: Highly influential technologist (Carmack) making a substantive, original argument about whether semiconductor fabrication could benefit from the same disruptive experimentation that SpaceX brought to space launch. Very high engagement (2084 likes, 95k views), profound implications for AI hardware/compute infrastructure
items.summary_html: John Carmack's detailed analysis comparing semiconductor fabrication to space launch before SpaceX - arguing fabs may be in a similar state of suboptimal conservatism where 'too big to fail' mentality prevents experimentation and improvement

items.title:
Hot take on Elon’s surprise decision to rent 30 megawatts of compute to Anthropic:

1. It’s a tacit ...
items.content:
Hot take on Elon’s surprise decision to rent 30 megawatts of compute to Anthropic:

1. It’s a tacit concession that xAI is not all that close to AGI (despite what he suggested last year).

2. It’s more evidence that pure scaling doesn’t get you to AGI.

3. If xAI turns out to be just another cloud company, in an increasingly crowded market, SpaceX investors very likely overpaid.

4. The very fact that xAI *has* an enough of oversupply to sell some to a big competitors doesn’t bode well for hyperscalers. We may very quickly find ourselves in a world where supply outstrips demands and prices drop precipitously. 

5. Customers win but …

6. Companies like Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft and Oracle could all take a hit, especially given what they have done to their cash flows to build up their cloud compute resources.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-05-07&category=reddit#item-3491b5b7a811) discussion, Gary Marcus provides a 6-point analysis of Elon Musk's decision to rent 30 megawatts of compute from xAI to Anthropic, arguing it signals xAI isn't close to AGI, scaling alone doesn't work, and oversupply may hurt hyperscalers.
items.reasoning: High engagement (138K views, 543 likes), original strategic analysis from prominent AI critic about a major industry development. Implications for cloud compute market and AGI timelines.

items.title:
A few major use cases for agentic coding for me:

1. Adhoc data visualizations. Anytime I have a que...
items.content:
A few major use cases for agentic coding for me:

1. Adhoc data visualizations. Anytime I have a question that can be answered quantitatively, I generate some code to make a plot.

2. Adhoc data annotation UIs. In ML, "make your own dataset" is often the answer, and that used to take a lot of custom UI work.

3. Adhoc CLIs for existing code. With visual elements.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet shares his top 3 use cases for agentic coding: ad-hoc data visualizations, data annotation UIs, and CLIs for existing code
items.reasoning: Practical insights from Keras creator on real-world agentic coding applications, good engagement (28K views), actionable and grounded perspective
items.summary_html: François Chollet shares his top 3 use cases for agentic coding: ad-hoc data visualizations, data annotation UIs, and CLIs for existing code
items.content_html:
A few major use cases for agentic coding for me:

1. Adhoc data visualizations. Anytime I have a question that can be answered quantitatively, I generate some code to make a plot.

2. Adhoc data annotation UIs. In ML, "make your own dataset" is often the answer, and that used to take a lot of custom UI work.

3. Adhoc CLIs for existing code. With visual elements.

items.title:
Elon renting his GPUs to Anthropic can mean only one thing (three-fold):

- There's no money in trai...
items.content:
Elon renting his GPUs to Anthropic can mean only one thing (three-fold):

- There's no money in training an underdog LLM and, while feasible, there's no money in trying to catch the leader.

- There's no money in selling token-based inference of yours or others' underdog LLMs.

- There's money in providing leaders and persistent underdogs with fat pockets a place to burn their cash.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov's main analysis: xAI renting GPUs to Anthropic means (1) no money in training underdog LLMs, (2) no money in selling inference of underdog LLMs, (3) money in being a compute provider for leaders.
items.reasoning: High engagement (872 likes, 111K views), original strategic analysis of major industry development. Clear thesis about compute market dynamics.
items.summary_html: Burkov's main analysis: xAI renting GPUs to Anthropic means (1) no money in training underdog LLMs, (2) no money in selling inference of underdog LLMs, (3) money in being a compute provider for leaders.
items.content_html:
Elon renting his GPUs to Anthropic can mean only one thing (three-fold):

  • There's no money in training an underdog LLM and, while feasible, there's no money in trying to catch the leader.
  • There's no money in selling token-based inference of yours or others' underdog LLMs.
  • There's money in providing leaders and persistent underdogs with fat pockets a place to burn their cash.

items.title:
Genesis AI pivoted hard this week from model lab to a full-stack robotics company.

The company rele...
items.content:
Genesis AI pivoted hard this week from model lab to a full-stack robotics company.

The company released its first foundation model, GENE-26.5, alongside a dexterous robotic hand and a data-capture glove.

GENE-26.5 can run a range of robots, including those by other manufacturers.

In a new demo, Genesis’ robotic hands: crack eggs, slice tomatoes, blend smoothies, work on a Rubik’s Cube, and play piano.

Genesis: "We are approaching the endgame for robotics."

items.title: @connor_beem I had dinner with Tom Gruber back when he worked for Apple. I think it was 2011. He's o...
items.content:
@connor_beem I had dinner with Tom Gruber back when he worked for Apple. I think it was 2011. He's one of three cofounders of Siri.

Asked him: "what are you learning now that you are at Apple?"

"Google is beating us."

"How do you know that?"

"We instrumented them and us and their AI is learning faster than ours is so we need to start over."

Budget never approved to rewrite it from scratch. Politics, committees, and egos, which are the same thing, are to blame.

The rewritten one arrives in June at WWDC.

items.summary: Scobleizer shares anecdote about dinner with Siri co-founder Tom Gruber circa 2011, revealing Apple knew Google's AI was learning faster. Claims Apple's rewritten Siri arrives at WWDC in June

items.title: “they hadn’t figured out how OpenAI would pay for it” may turn out to be the epitaph for an entire e...

items.title:
How do NLAs work?

An NLA consists of two models. One converts activations into text. The other trie...
items.content:
How do NLAs work?

An NLA consists of two models. One converts activations into text. The other tries to reconstruct activations from this text. We train the models together to make this reconstruction accurate.

This incentivizes the text to capture what’s in the activation. https://t.co/122rkJwYH7
items.author: @AnthropicAI
items.summary: Anthropic explains NLA mechanism: two models work together - one converts activations to text, another reconstructs activations from text, trained jointly for accurate reconstruction.
items.reasoning: Technical explanation of novel interpretability method. Clear description of architecture.
items.summary_html: Anthropic explains NLA mechanism: two models work together - one converts activations to text, another reconstructs activations from text, trained jointly for accurate reconstruction.
items.content_html:
How do NLAs work?

An NLA consists of two models. One converts activations into text. The other tries to reconstruct activations from this text. We train the models together to make this reconstruction accurate.

This incentivizes the text to capture what’s in the activation. https://t.co/122rkJwYH7

items.title: New course: Build agents that respond to users with not only plaintext, but custom UIs like charts, ...
items.content:
New course: Build agents that respond to users with not only plaintext, but custom UIs like charts, forms, and whiteboards, generated on demand and displayed right in the chat. This short course is built in partnership with  @CopilotKit and taught by @ataiiam, co-founder of CopilotKit.

You'll learn three approaches: Your agent can pick from custom components you build, like charts and forms. It can compose new layouts from a set of building blocks you provide, like rows, cards, and text. Or it can incorporate existing third-party apps, like a whiteboard or a calendar, right inside the conversation.

Skills you’ll gain:
- Build agents that render custom components like charts and forms on demand
- Build an app where the agent and user collaborate on shared data, beyond just the chat window
- Place third-party apps like maps, calendars, and whiteboards right in your interface

Join and build agents that give users something to see and act on! https://t.co/lvMy0YdF3z

For instance, we find that NLAs help discover hidden motivations in an intentionally misaligned model. https://t.co/NJ5yc8p7Dn

items.title:
Mamba 3 is a non-Transformer LM architecture.

Unlike attention, which maintains a growing KV cache ...
items.content:
Mamba 3 is a non-Transformer LM architecture.

Unlike attention, which maintains a growing KV cache that scales with sequence length, Mamba-3 compresses all past context into a fixed-size state of a fixed dimensionality​.

Each decode step therefore takes constant time regardless of how long the sequence is, making total decoding cost linear in sequence length.

The performance on the 1.5B parameter scale is comparable to Transformer with much faster inference with context sizes above 16k (see the table below).

Read this ICLR 2026 Outstanding Paper runner-up with an AI tutor: https://t.co/wBLarw0jmK

PDF: https://t.co/y5Xc7cUeGf
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses Mamba 3, an ICLR 2026 Outstanding Paper runner-up, highlighting its fixed-size state for constant decode time per step and linear total decoding cost, with performance comparable to Transformers at 1.5B parameters.
items.reasoning: Technical content about important non-Transformer architecture receiving top academic recognition. Moderate engagement. Relevant to future of model architectures.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses Mamba 3, an ICLR 2026 Outstanding Paper runner-up, highlighting its fixed-size state for constant decode time per step and linear total decoding cost, with performance comparable to Transformers at 1.5B parameters.

items.title: @fleetingbits @readsail Some rambling responses. Realistically if you're going to get access you nee...
items.content:
@fleetingbits @readsail Some rambling responses. Realistically if you're going to get access you need to be respectful. It's fairly obvious that part of my job is I need to meet these people and understand them in the context of the models they build.

I write and speak from how I feel, this isn't new, this post fits in with how I've been working for years. Not much new here.

Things like this (not sure what's left keeps getting deleted/edited?)

6) so, you should ask "how are your teams organized?", "what is the relationship between the commercial side and the research side?", "how do you divide compute between research and inference?"

7) "how much compute do you allocate per researcher?" "how do you decide whether to hire more researchers or give existing researchers more compute?", "how do you get research ideas?", etc...

8) "where do you purchase your chips from?", "how do you finance chip purchases?", "do you use a cloud provider, colocation or your own datacenter, why?", etc...

We ask, the answers aren't very surprising or interesting. Very standard, but also most of the researchers we talk to are like "not my decision" and when you talk to team leads they know how to fillibuster and not give clear answers. Much like you would expect in the US, as I've done a lot of let work to get people to explain frontier lab org structure -- very cagey.

Some information I got outside of official channels at companies I'm not just going to drop in a blog post to tens of thousands of people if I don't think it's fair or useful.

Of course all writing should be taken in the context of the person, I'm but one source, but I think you're largely asking for something that can't exist, unless you're just trying to get a leak pipeline out of more companies.

Enjoy your trip to china!

items.title:
What if every decode step gave the next one a head start?

Meet Guess-Verify-Refine — a new hardware...
items.content:
What if every decode step gave the next one a head start?

Meet Guess-Verify-Refine — a new hardware-aware sparse-attention algorithm from NVIDIA Research. Built for TensorRT LLM on Blackwell, it reuses temporal patterns across decode steps for:

→ 1.88x faster Top-K attention
→ 9.3% better end-to-end latency in low-latency serving

Dive into the paper: https://t.co/quu7wX9sCh
items.author: @NVIDIAAI
items.summary: NVIDIA Research introduces Guess-Verify-Refine, a hardware-aware sparse-attention algorithm for TensorRT LLM on Blackwell achieving 1.88x faster Top-K attention and 9.3% better end-to-end latency.
items.reasoning: Technical paper from NVIDIA Research with practical inference speedups. Relevant to model serving efficiency on latest hardware.
items.summary_html: NVIDIA Research introduces Guess-Verify-Refine, a hardware-aware sparse-attention algorithm for TensorRT LLM on Blackwell achieving 1.88x faster Top-K attention and 9.3% better end-to-end latency.
items.content_html:
What if every decode step gave the next one a head start?

Meet Guess-Verify-Refine — a new hardware-aware sparse-attention algorithm from NVIDIA Research. Built for TensorRT LLM on Blackwell, it reuses temporal patterns across decode steps for:

→ 1.88x faster Top-K attention

→ 9.3% better end-to-end latency in low-latency serving

Dive into the paper: https://t.co/quu7wX9sCh

items.title:
You can now run deep research on your laptop without OpenAI.

Most AI research tools send your queri...
items.content:
You can now run deep research on your laptop without OpenAI.

Most AI research tools send your queries to the cloud.
 
Your data leaves the machine the moment you hit search.
 
Local Deep Research flips that model entirely.
 
It's an open-source assistant that runs fully on your device.
 
Ask a complex question and it searches the web, academic papers, and your private documents at once.
 
The system synthesizes everything into a cited report.
 
It hits ~95% on the SimpleQA benchmark.
 
That matches state-of-the-art commercial agents.
 
Three modes cover most workflows:
 
1. Quick summary in 30 seconds
2. Detailed analysis with structured findings
3. Full report generation
 
A new LangGraph strategy autonomously decides which engines to query, from arXiv to PubMed.
 
Each user gets a SQLCipher database with AES-256 encryption.
 
No telemetry runs in the background.
 
Pair it with Ollama and nothing ever leaves your laptop.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Local Deep Research: open-source tool that runs deep research locally on your laptop. Uses LangGraph to query arXiv, PubMed, web. Claims ~95% on SimpleQA benchmark. SQLCipher encryption, works with Ollama.
items.reasoning: Practical open-source tool for privacy-focused AI research assistants. Good technical detail and addresses real privacy concerns. Moderate engagement suggests community interest.
items.summary_html: Local Deep Research: open-source tool that runs deep research locally on your laptop. Uses LangGraph to query arXiv, PubMed, web. Claims ~95% on SimpleQA benchmark. SQLCipher encryption, works with Ollama.
items.content_html:
You can now run deep research on your laptop without OpenAI.

Most AI research tools send your queries to the cloud.

Your data leaves the machine the moment you hit search.

Local Deep Research flips that model entirely.

It's an open-source assistant that runs fully on your device.

Ask a complex question and it searches the web, academic papers, and your private documents at once.

The system synthesizes everything into a cited report.

It hits ~95% on the SimpleQA benchmark.

That matches state-of-the-art commercial agents.

Three modes cover most workflows:

1. Quick summary in 30 seconds

2. Detailed analysis with structured findings

3. Full report generation

A new LangGraph strategy autonomously decides which engines to query, from arXiv to PubMed.

Each user gets a SQLCipher database with AES-256 encryption.

No telemetry runs in the background.

Pair it with Ollama and nothing ever leaves your laptop.

items.title:
Agentic coding is as much about the harness as it is about the model.

The greatest and latest model...
items.content:
Agentic coding is as much about the harness as it is about the model.

The greatest and latest model in a mediocre harness will suck.

Here are the things you can do with the integration between Junie CLI and PyCharm:

• Semantic rename that handles scope, overloads, and same-name symbols correctly, because the CLI uses the IDE's full project index

• The CLI can build and test your project through the IDE's pre-configured runners. No need for explanations in markdown files

• You can do a synonym-aware search across the indexed codebase. This is much more powerful than grep searches

• Open a file or select text in your IDE, and the CLI knows it immediately

Honestly, this is a joy to use.

Obviously, JetBrains is uniquely positioned to implement such a deep integration between all of its tools.

Here is the link to the Junie CLI: https://t.co/629vl5eLHF
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama describing JetBrains Junie CLI features: semantic rename, IDE-integrated build/test, synonym-aware search, bidirectional file context with PyCharm
items.reasoning: Detailed technical overview of agentic coding tool with deep IDE integration. Highlights important trend of coding agents gaining access to full IDE capabilities rather than just text. From established ML educator.
items.summary_html: Santiago Valdarrama describing JetBrains Junie CLI features: semantic rename, IDE-integrated build/test, synonym-aware search, bidirectional file context with PyCharm
items.content_html:
Agentic coding is as much about the harness as it is about the model.

The greatest and latest model in a mediocre harness will suck.

Here are the things you can do with the integration between Junie CLI and PyCharm:

• Semantic rename that handles scope, overloads, and same-name symbols correctly, because the CLI uses the IDE's full project index

• The CLI can build and test your project through the IDE's pre-configured runners. No need for explanations in markdown files

• You can do a synonym-aware search across the indexed codebase. This is much more powerful than grep searches

• Open a file or select text in your IDE, and the CLI knows it immediately

Honestly, this is a joy to use.

Obviously, JetBrains is uniquely positioned to implement such a deep integration between all of its tools.

Here is the link to the Junie CLI: https://t.co/629vl5eLHF

items.title:
His AI is one of only two I've trusted my life directly to. 

@elonmusk already hinted at us that a ...
items.content:
His AI is one of only two I've trusted my life directly to. 

@elonmusk already hinted at us that a new pattern is arriving. 

Gather posts on X. I like lists, but there will be a variety of ways.
Analyze them. 
Produce script. 
Give it to Grok to turn into TV show.
Post.

Got me to think about what X will be like in five years.

"Hey Optimus can you tell me the news." 

And it knows everything about what you need to really know.

Change is ahead.

New Apple Siri comes in June. 

So who will aim datacenters full of NVIDIA cards at Apple?

:-)

items.title:
Huge leap in video generation!

Look at the faces here.

For the first time, we have a tool that doe...
items.content:
Huge leap in video generation!

Look at the faces here.

For the first time, we have a tool that doesn't change characters' faces between scenes.

This is BACH.

Characters with the same bone structure, same skin tone, same eyes across every single clip.

Watch the close-ups.

items.title: They lie. AI is just a convenient reason to cut a large batch of positions and avoid complaints and ...
items.content:
They lie. AI is just a convenient reason to cut a large batch of positions and avoid complaints and investigations.

Most of those who make decisions to fire "due to AI" wouldn't be able to find ChatGPT on Google.

They fire the least-performing workers to achieve economies. IT workers are overpriced. I once met a web developer whose salary was twice mine, while I was a Ph.D. in AI. These companies will either rehire some of them at much lower salaries or hire someone else even cheaper.

items.title:
AI just learned to predict the future, not just react to it. 

A new paper just cracked open how neu...
items.content:
AI just learned to predict the future, not just react to it. 

A new paper just cracked open how neural networks learn to predict.
 
Researchers trained recurrent neural networks to chase a moving target.
 
Some learned to react. Others learned to anticipate.
 
The difference came down to one thing: the size of the neural code.
 
Small networks chased where the target was right now.
 
Large networks predicted where it would be next.
 
Both caught the target. Only the larger ones showed real foresight.
 
In looped environments, the bigger nets waited at the spot the target would reappear.
 
Live mice trained on the same task did the exact same thing.
 
What separates reactive from predictive behavior:
 
> Reactive nets track distance only
> Predictive nets build world maps
> High dimensions unlock anticipation
> Low dimensions stay greedy
 
Intelligence may need room to breathe.
 
Capacity is what turns reflex into planning.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Research paper summary: RNNs trained to chase moving targets develop predictive vs reactive behavior based on network size. Larger networks build world models and anticipate, matching behavior observed in live mice.
items.reasoning: Interesting neuroscience/AI research connecting network capacity to predictive behavior, with biological validation. However, it's a secondary summary from a newsletter account, and the framing is hyperbolic ('AI learned to predict the future').
items.summary_html: Research paper summary: RNNs trained to chase moving targets develop predictive vs reactive behavior based on network size. Larger networks build world models and anticipate, matching behavior observed in live mice.
items.content_html:
AI just learned to predict the future, not just react to it.

A new paper just cracked open how neural networks learn to predict.

Researchers trained recurrent neural networks to chase a moving target.

Some learned to react. Others learned to anticipate.

The difference came down to one thing: the size of the neural code.

Small networks chased where the target was right now.

Large networks predicted where it would be next.

Both caught the target. Only the larger ones showed real foresight.

In looped environments, the bigger nets waited at the spot the target would reappear.

Live mice trained on the same task did the exact same thing.

What separates reactive from predictive behavior:

> Reactive nets track distance only

> Predictive nets build world maps

> High dimensions unlock anticipation

> Low dimensions stay greedy

Intelligence may need room to breathe.

Capacity is what turns reflex into planning.

items.title: I am discovering that when you are still in the *very* early stages of your startup, it’s *extremely...
items.content: I am discovering that when you are still in the *very* early stages of your startup, it’s *extremely* valuable to have a well-thought-out manifesto written and available, where you dish out not only the problems that you are trying to work on, but also your general work philosophy. That way when someone wants to “jump on a quick call” you can send them to read it first. This is a great filtering mechanism that eliminates 99%+ of all grifters and NPCs.

items.title: A new curriculum on @ChapterPal : **Prep reading for the LLM finetuning and alignment techniques int...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal : **Prep reading for the LLM finetuning and alignment techniques interview**

Curated by Andriy Burkov

This curriculum provides a comprehensive progression through the theoretical foundations and practical methodologies of large language model (LLM) finetuning and alignment.

Learners begin by exploring core concepts in instruction tuning and data-efficient alignment techniques like LIMA, LoRA, and QLoRA, which enable high-performance model adaptation with minimal resource requirements.

The series then shifts focus to various alignment strategies, including reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), constitutional AI, and preference optimization methods like DPO, KTO, and ORPO.

Beyond standard alignment, the curriculum covers advanced topics such as iterative reasoning, process-based verification, model evaluation using LLM-as-a-judge, and adversarial robustness.

By synthesizing these papers, students will gain a deep understanding of how to transform foundation models into instruction-following assistants that are reliable, steerable, and compliant with human preferences.

https://t.co/RIM6mc62WX
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a new curriculum on LLM finetuning and alignment techniques covering RLHF, DPO, LoRA, QLoRA, Constitutional AI, and other methods.
items.reasoning: Educational resource from ML textbook author but low engagement. Useful compilation but not novel.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a new curriculum on LLM finetuning and alignment techniques covering RLHF, DPO, LoRA, QLoRA, Constitutional AI, and other methods.

Learners begin by exploring core concepts in instruction tuning and data-efficient alignment techniques like LIMA, LoRA, and QLoRA, which enable high-performance model adaptation with minimal resource requirements.

The series then shifts focus to various alignment strategies, including reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), constitutional AI, and preference optimization methods like DPO, KTO, and ORPO.

Beyond standard alignment, the curriculum covers advanced topics such as iterative reasoning, process-based verification, model evaluation using LLM-as-a-judge, and adversarial robustness.

By synthesizing these papers, students will gain a deep understanding of how to transform foundation models into instruction-following assistants that are reliable, steerable, and compliant with human preferences.

We’re moving from “ask AI anything”

→ to “AI understands everything around you.”

That’s a fundamen...
items.content:
We’re moving from “ask AI anything”

→ to “AI understands everything around you.”

That’s a fundamental interface shift.

If you’re building products, this changes UX, data strategy, and distribution.

I break this down in this latest video with Meta.

Curious how you see this playing out,

What’s the first real-world use case where “AI that sees” becomes indispensable?

items.title:
What does generative transformation look like beyond adopting AI tools?

It’s not more pilots.
It’s ...
items.content:
What does generative transformation look like beyond adopting AI tools?

It’s not more pilots.
It’s not more tools.

It’s building a system that compounds value.
At @IBM Think, a few takeaways from my conversation with Blue Polaris:
→ A clear “North Star” that aligns every AI initiative
→ Governance and trust as prerequisites
→ 10x ROI means nothing if you can’t scale
→ A shift from one-off projects to scalable platforms

And the underrated edge? Community. The IBM Partner ecosystem is accelerating learning in real time.
Generative transformation is a discipline.
Watch our full conversation: https://t.co/5lJtJ4sfw8

#IBMPartner #Think26 #IBMPartnerPlus #BluePolaris

items.summary: Helen Yu shares takeaways from IBM Think conference about generative AI transformation strategy

Thermonuclear Tunnel of Ions 

Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) is at the forefront of groundbreaking nuclear fusion research, striving to replicate the extraordinary conditions found in the core of the Sun. Recreating an artificial sun where fusion takes place naturally at a staggering temperature of approximately 15 million centigrade. Fahrenheit 26 million. This ambitious endeavor holds the potential to revolutionize energy production and pave the way for a sustainable future.

High performance computing captured the light with superconducting manget and neutron sources

Here's what they've achieved so far to capture the light and recreate the nuclear fusion.

•   ITER (France): Targeting 150 million °C (Goal -
2035)
• SPARC (MIT): Also aims to hit 100+ million °C
(Goal - 2025-2026).
• DIII (San Diego): Tokamak plasma has
reached over 100 million in plasma (Already
reached)

The second shift is happening quietly, but it’s massive.

Multi-agent AI.

Instead of one system doing everything:

→ One agent plans your trip
→ One compares destinations
→ One finds activities

Parallel intelligence.

This is how AI starts to feel less like a tool and more like a team.

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical compon...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.

Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.

Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.

Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode  

References

Jeon, S., Kim, M., Yoon, J., & et al. (2023). Machine learning-based obesity classification considering 3D body scanner measurements. Scientific Reports, 13, 3299. Retrieved February 27, 2025, from https://t.co/nWc1LxIuf1

items.title:
The real leap isn’t better answers.

It’s multimodal reasoning.

→ AI that sees images
→ Understands...
items.content:
The real leap isn’t better answers.

It’s multimodal reasoning.

→ AI that sees images
→ Understands context
→ Makes decisions instantly

Example:

Point your phone at food → it suggests the healthiest option
Scan a product → it gives a real comparison, not biased reviews

This moves AI from reactive to situationally aware.

Real-world signals.

And with AI glasses coming…

It won’t just respond to your world.
It will live inside it.

items.title: Collaboration has always shaped human progress, and this becomes even more evident when we look at h...
items.content:
Collaboration has always shaped human progress, and this becomes even more evident when we look at how technology evolves.

The recent step by @Huawei, together with LG and Nokia, to join Sisvel’s PoS patent pool reflects a direction that is worth attention, where the role of Huawei as a founding member reflects both the wide recognition of its technologies by the industry and a long-term commitment to technology sharing and open innovation.

It shows how innovation gains real value when it moves beyond individual ownership and becomes accessible within a shared framework.

Technology, in fact, is not meant to remain confined, but to be applied, extended, and made available so that others can build on it. In this sense, simplifying access to standard essential patents for connected POS devices is not just a technical or legal improvement, but a way to create the conditions for broader participation and faster development across the ecosystem.

From a market perspective, this approach can reduce complexity for companies developing payment solutions, support the evolution of POS systems into more capable connected platforms, and make it easier for new players to enter the space, while progressively shaping a more efficient, one-stop licensing model as more licensors join the ecosystem.

There is also a broader message here. When companies align around common mechanisms, they create a foundation that benefits the entire value chain, which is particularly relevant in areas such as digital payments where interoperability and scale are key.

This direction is consistent with Huawei’s broader track record in enabling access to innovation. Over time, the company has contributed to multiple patent pools and initiatives, including:

- its participation in the Cellular IoT program, making technologies such as LTE-M and NB-IoT available globally

- its role in advancing Wi-Fi standards and related patent contributions

- a portfolio of over 260 license agreements signed by the end of 2025.

The pool has also recently welcomed additional members, including NEC, BlackBerry, Datang Mobile, KPN, Wilus, JVCKENWOOD, and SK Telecom.

In the end, progress in technology is rarely the result of isolated efforts but grows through collaboration, shared standards, and the ability to work together toward common goals.

In partnership with @Huawei.

#DigitalPayments #FinTech #Sisvel #Huawei

items.title:
Amazing work

I almost got rid of all plastic in my house

Last few things are a reverse osmosis fil...
items.content:
Amazing work

I almost got rid of all plastic in my house

Last few things are a reverse osmosis filter, right now we drink from the biggest water bags we can find 10L / 2.6 gallon

They are plastic but high volume so less surface per liter

Tap water is shown to have microplastics too, so reverse osmosis is the solution I think

Soon!

items.title: So we cook almost completely at home, locally farmed free roaming cow and chicken with sea view and ...
items.content:
So we cook almost completely at home, locally farmed free roaming cow and chicken with sea view and local farm vegetables and mostly local fruit 

Imported Brazilian premium coffee beans from Sul de Minas, ground ourselves and then pour over black coffee with stainless steel Hario V60 in ceramic mugs

Glass or wooden containers

No plastics anywhere

When I fly I think next time I'll just make slices of steak with rice and vegetables because it makes me feel better than whatever slop they serve me on the plane even in business class!

I agree food outside is mostly contaminated microplastics soy slop now
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio describes his food philosophy: locally farmed meat, no plastics, home cooking, premium coffee setup. Plans to bring homemade food on flights.
items.reasoning: Off-topic lifestyle content, not AI-related

Glass or wooden containers

No plastics anywhere

When I fly I think next time I'll just make slices of steak with rice and vegetables because it makes me feel better than whatever slop they serve me on the plane even in business class!

items.title:
GaN - GA - Gallium Nitride Processor. Ft.@gp_pulipaka by @Analyticsindiam 

This is how Gallium look...
items.content:
GaN - GA - Gallium Nitride Processor. Ft.@gp_pulipaka by @Analyticsindiam 

This is how Gallium looks like.  The process of invention dates back to the year, 1874.  An ancient periodic element from the periodic table that we need to check. 

It has an atomic mass of 70 u with a melting point at 85.57 F. Gallium can form a number of compounds. Chief among them are: Gallium arsenide (GaA), Gallium nitride (GaN), and Gallium phosphide (GP).
